reasoning_summary: The fresh refactor baseline run 20260421_133418_bf16_gemm_v1_c859cd06 measured 24.408 ms, which is still about 1.87 ms slower than the local cuBLASLt reference at roughly 22.29 ms. The custom kernel already carries 16.62 active warps, so the dominant gap is not raw occupancy versus the vendor path; it is the much larger synchronization and latency cost inside the current hot-band 128x128 PTX microkernel. The custom profile shows 5.49% barrier stalls and 7.19% long-scoreboard stalls, while the cuBLAS reference stays around 0.16% barrier and 0.19% long-scoreboard on the same workload. That makes the best next move a low-risk launch swap to the already-implemented 128x128x32 staged hot-band kernel, followed by a deeper rewrite that collapses the current PTX microkernel wait/sync cadence, with the 64x384 row-band split rebalance held as the third lever because the old autotune sweep already identified 384-wide hot-band tiling as the strongest measured shape family.
notes: No additional human-review-only idea family is queued yet on this clean refactor branch. For round 1/10 the ranking stays tightly coupled to the live profile: test the existing 128x128x32 staged hot-band sibling first, keep the PTX wait-cadence rewrite as the higher-risk second lever, and reserve the 64x384 split rebalance as the third alternative.

directions.name: Promote The Existing 128x128x32 Staged Hot-Band Kernel
directions.hypothesis: The current default hot-band kernel is paying too much synchronization and wait overhead per 16-wide K tile. The existing 128x128x32 staged sibling already consumes two K tiles per stage and should reduce barrier and long-scoreboard pressure in the dominant hot-band region without changing the overall 128x128 CTA footprint.
directions.expected_bottleneck: synchronization_barrier_issue and long_scoreboard latency in the current hot-band 128x128 PTX microkernel
directions.risk: Low-to-moderate risk: the kernel already exists, but the alternate staging schedule may trade barrier relief for higher shared-memory traffic or a small register increase.
ranking_notes: This is the cheapest measured-stall hypothesis to test because the alternative kernel body is already in-tree.
It keeps the same hot-band CTA shape while directly changing the per-stage wait and barrier cadence.
directions.stop_condition: Stop if runtime does not improve by at least 0.3 ms or if barrier and long-scoreboard stalls stay flat within measurement noise.
expected_local_changes: Swap the default fixed-shape hot-band launch from the PTX microkernel path to the existing 128x128x32 staged kernel.
Keep the residual 64x384 row-band and 64x96 tail unchanged for the first measurement.

directions.name: Collapse The PTX Microkernel Wait-And-Sync Cadence
directions.hypothesis: The current PTX hot-band loop calls cp.async wait plus a CTA sync after every K tile, which matches the observed 5.49% barrier and 7.19% long-scoreboard cost. Porting a pairwise or two-K stage advance pattern, similar to the peeled 64x384 helper, should reduce the synchronization tax while keeping the current microkernel math path.
directions.expected_bottleneck: per-tile cp.async wait plus CTA barrier cadence in the PTX hot-band microkernel
directions.risk: Moderate-to-high risk: this edits the PTX hot-band steady-state pipeline directly, so a small synchronization mistake can break correctness or regress tensor feed rate.
ranking_notes: The custom-vs-cuBLAS gap is dominated by synchronization and scoreboard stalls, so this has the clearest direct path to the remaining ~1.9 ms gap.
It ranks behind dir_01 only because the existing staged sibling provides a cheaper first measurement.
directions.stop_condition: Stop if the edited loop reduces barrier stalls but causes correctness failures, or if runtime regresses by more than 0.5 ms after the first buildable attempt.
expected_local_changes: Refactor the PTX microkernel steady-state loop so future-stage refill happens in a pairwise cadence instead of waiting and syncing after every single K tile.
Reuse the existing peeled-row-band stage-advance helper pattern where possible to keep the sync semantics auditable.

directions.name: Push More Hot-Band Rows Into The 64x384 Peeled Path
directions.hypothesis: The historical fixed-main-tile sweep already found 64x384 to be the strongest hot-band width family. Rebalancing the fixed split so fewer rows use the 128x128 PTX hotspot and more rows use the peeled 64x384 path may reduce CTA scheduling and synchronization overhead without rewriting the microkernel itself.
directions.expected_bottleneck: hot-band decomposition choice is leaving too much work on the slower 128x128 PTX hotspot instead of the best historical 384-wide family
directions.risk: Moderate risk: the old tile sweep favored 384 width, but expanding that path changes the row split and could expose new register or launch overhead outside the tiny residual band it currently covers.
ranking_notes: The 64x384 family is supported by old fixed-tile measurements, but the current profile still points first at the 128x128 PTX hotspot, so this ranks third.
This is still a good fallback if the staged 128x128 sibling and PTX wait-cadence rewrite both fail to close the gap.
directions.stop_condition: Stop if the rebalance improves the profiled kernel metrics but total runtime does not move, which would imply the split change only shifted work rather than removing the dominant bottleneck.
expected_local_changes: Retune kFixedPivotHotRows so a larger slice of the hot band launches through the peeled 64x384 path.
Keep the 64x96 tail kernel unchanged while measuring whether the split rebalance alone helps.

reasoning_summary: Round 1 already falsified the simplest barrier-reduction hypothesis: swapping the default hot-band launch to the existing 128x128x32 staged sibling pushed runtime from 24.408 ms to 30.008 ms, increased barrier stalls from 5.49% to 10.09%, and raised registers per thread from 200 to 212. That means the next round should not keep exploring deeper K staging on top of the regressed head. The accepted base remains the 24.408 ms PTX hot-band launch, and the better next move is to reduce how much work flows through that hotspot rather than making its stages even heavier. The strongest measured family evidence still points at the 64x384 hot-band path from the fixed-tile sweep, so the top-ranked direction is to restore the accepted base and expand the peeled 64x384 row-band share. The second direction is a smaller launch-order change inside the accepted PTX path by retuning kFixedHotBandPtxGroupedRows. The third direction keeps the same 128x128 single-K staging depth but swaps away from the microkernel-specific accumulate path to the existing non-microkernel sibling. The cuBLAS reference remains useful as a ceiling: it tolerates similar register pressure yet keeps barrier stalls near zero, so the custom search should focus on reducing barrier exposure and wasted hot-band scheduling work, not on adding more shared-memory staging.
notes: Round 2 should branch from the accepted base run 20260421_133418_bf16_gemm_v1_c859cd06, not from the round-1 regressed staged-kernel head. The top priority is to reduce time spent in the 128x128 hotspot rather than add more K-stage depth there.

directions.name: Restore The Accepted Base And Expand The 64x384 Row Band
directions.hypothesis: The accepted PTX hot-band launch is still the best measured baseline, but too much of the fixed shape remains on the 128x128 hotspot. Restoring that baseline and reducing kFixedPivotHotRows should move more work into the historically stronger 64x384 peeled path, cutting aggregate barrier exposure without reintroducing the staged-128x128 regression.
directions.expected_bottleneck: too much of the fixed hot band is assigned to the 128x128 PTX hotspot instead of the stronger 64x384 family
directions.risk: Moderate risk: the historical 64x384 sweep predates the newest PTX hot-band refinements, so moving too many rows may simply shift the bottleneck instead of shrinking it.
ranking_notes: Round 1 ruled out the staged 128x128 branch, so the next move should start from the accepted PTX baseline rather than stack another edit on the regressed head.
The 64x384 family is the only alternative with direct measured support in the repo state.
directions.stop_condition: Stop if runtime returns to the accepted base but does not improve it by at least 0.2 ms, or if barrier stalls fall while total runtime stays flat.
expected_local_changes: Restore the default fixed-shape hot-band launch from the regressed 128x128x32 staged sibling back to the accepted PTX hot-band kernel.
Lower kFixedPivotHotRows so more hot-band rows run through the 64x384 peeled path while keeping the 64x96 tail unchanged.

directions.name: Retune PTX Hot-Band Grouped-Row Traversal
directions.hypothesis: The accepted PTX hot-band kernel may be paying unnecessary launch-order and cache-reuse penalties from the current four-row grouped traversal. Retuning kFixedHotBandPtxGroupedRows changes the block issue pattern without reopening the heavy staged-kernel path.
directions.expected_bottleneck: launch-order and cache/locality inefficiency inside the accepted PTX hot-band traversal
directions.risk: Low-to-moderate risk: the edit is small, but the effect can easily be noise-sized if grouped traversal is not actually the main issue.
ranking_notes: This is the cheapest clean experiment on top of the accepted PTX path.
It ranks behind dir_01 because the historical sweep gives stronger support to the 64x384 family than to any specific grouped-row value.
directions.stop_condition: Stop if runtime change is within measurement noise after one buildable attempt.
expected_local_changes: Keep the accepted PTX hot-band launch in place.
Retune kFixedHotBandPtxGroupedRows and measure whether CTA issue order and locality improve.

directions.name: Swap To The Single-K 128x128 Non-Microkernel Sibling
directions.hypothesis: The existing single-K 128x128 sibling keeps the same stage depth as the accepted PTX path but uses the regular accumulate helper instead of the microkernel-specific ordering. That may trim scheduler or register overhead without paying the staged-128x128x32 cost.
directions.expected_bottleneck: microkernel-specific accumulate/store scheduling inside the accepted 128x128 hot-band path
directions.risk: Moderate risk: this is a variant swap without direct performance evidence on the current refactor branch, so it can regress just as easily as it improves.
ranking_notes: This is a controlled kernel-variant swap with less shared-memory expansion than the staged 128x128x32 path.
It ranks third because there is no direct in-repo measurement yet showing the regular 128x128 sibling beats the accepted PTX microkernel.
directions.stop_condition: Stop if registers climb or active-warps fall relative to the accepted PTX base without a runtime improvement.
expected_local_changes: Restore the accepted PTX baseline if needed, then replace the default hot-band launch with the single-K non-microkernel 128x128 sibling.
Keep the residual 64x384 path and 64x96 tail unchanged for the first comparison.

reasoning_summary: Round 2 falsified the row-band expansion family much more strongly than the raw PTX kernel profile alone suggests. The profiled 128x128 PTX hotspot itself improved in almost every local metric versus the round-1 staged regression: runtime-facing tensor activity rose to 48.45%, barrier stalls dropped to 5.98%, registers fell to 198, and compute/memory throughput both improved. Yet total end-to-end runtime exploded to 47.291 ms. That means the extra 64x384 peeled work is far more expensive than the NCU-selected hotspot implies, so the search should stop expanding that family and return to the accepted base. The next best move is therefore a cheap, isolated launch-order tweak on top of the accepted PTX hot-band path: retune kFixedHotBandPtxGroupedRows to change how many row tiles share the same hot-band B tile traversal. The second-best move keeps the same row split but swaps to the existing single-K 128x128 non-microkernel sibling, isolating whether the microkernel-specific accumulate ordering is hurting scoreboard behavior. The third move is a more speculative launch-bounds retune to see whether the compiler can trim register pressure further without spills, but it ranks lower because the cuBLAS reference already shows that lower active-warps alone is not the main gap.
notes: Round 3 should return to the accepted base run 20260421_133418_bf16_gemm_v1_c859cd06 before implementing any new direction. The 64x384 row-band expansion family is now explicitly deprioritized after the 47.291 ms regression.

directions.name: Restore The Accepted Base And Increase PTX Grouped-Row Depth
directions.hypothesis: The accepted PTX hot-band kernel is still the fastest measured family, but its grouped-row traversal may not be feeding B-tile reuse aggressively enough. Restoring the accepted base and increasing kFixedHotBandPtxGroupedRows from 4 to 8 should keep more adjacent row tiles on the same hot-band B tile before advancing columns, which may reduce scoreboard waste without reopening the failed 64x384 expansion.
directions.expected_bottleneck: launch-order and B-tile reuse inefficiency inside the accepted PTX hot-band traversal
directions.risk: Low-to-moderate risk: the change is isolated, but a larger grouped-row depth can easily help B reuse while hurting A locality.
ranking_notes: The accepted PTX base still beats every attempted variant in this refactor loop, so the next move should be a small perturbation on that path.
Grouped-row depth is the cleanest way to change CTA issue order and B reuse without touching the K-stage structure.
directions.stop_condition: Stop if runtime change stays within noise or if long-scoreboard stalls rise further after the first measurement.
expected_local_changes: Restore the accepted PTX hot-band baseline by undoing the round-2 pivot-row rebalance.
Increase kFixedHotBandPtxGroupedRows from 4 to 8 while keeping the default PTX hot-band launch and 64x96 tail intact.

directions.name: Restore The Accepted Base And Swap To The Single-K 128x128 Sibling
directions.hypothesis: The existing single-K 128x128 sibling preserves the accepted row split and one-K staging depth while using the regular accumulate ordering instead of the microkernel-specific path. That may improve scoreboard behavior without paying the staged-128x128x32 penalty or the 64x384 row-band penalty.
directions.expected_bottleneck: microkernel-specific accumulate ordering is contributing to scoreboard overhead on the accepted hot-band split
directions.risk: Moderate risk: the variant is in-tree but still lacks a direct measurement on the accepted row split in this refactor loop.
ranking_notes: This is the most contained kernel-variant swap that stays on the accepted row split.
It ranks behind grouped-row retuning because the launch-order experiment is cheaper and more directly tied to the current scoreboard signal.
directions.stop_condition: Stop if runtime regresses again while register pressure and active-warps do not materially improve.
expected_local_changes: Restore the accepted PTX hot-band baseline row split.
Replace the default hot-band launch with the existing single-K 128x128 non-microkernel sibling.

directions.name: Restore The Accepted Base And Tighten PTX Launch Bounds
directions.hypothesis: The accepted PTX hotspot still sits around 200 registers per thread with occupancy capped at two blocks per SM. Tightening __launch_bounds__ to target three blocks per SM may trim enough register pressure to change latency hiding, provided the compiler does not spill heavily.
directions.expected_bottleneck: register pressure and low CTA residency on the accepted PTX hot-band path
directions.risk: High risk: forcing tighter launch bounds can introduce spills or a weaker instruction schedule, and the cuBLAS reference suggests occupancy alone is not the full answer.
ranking_notes: This is still worth keeping because the PTX path remains latency-limited.
It ranks third because the cuBLAS ceiling does not point to occupancy as the main missing ingredient.
directions.stop_condition: Stop immediately if build output or NCU shows spills, or if runtime regresses without reducing register pressure.
expected_local_changes: Restore the accepted PTX hot-band baseline row split.
Retune the PTX microkernel launch-bounds annotation and leave the rest of the launch topology unchanged for the first measurement.
